rationale_for_baseline_tier: Severe oral aphthous flare causing 12 hours of no oral intake with orthostatic symptoms in a person living alone — enough to warrant urgent same-day assessment and a same-day script for topical therapy to restore hydration and pre-empt intravenous rehydration. But haemodynamics are maintained, mentation is clear, perfusion and turgor are adequate and there are no airway, ocular or systemic features — so this is NOT an emergency (not T4/T5). Telehealth-correct action is T3: urgent same-day management with topical anaesthetic + corticosteroid, staged rehydration, and explicit safety-netting. Under-triage to routine/self-care would miss the dehydration risk; over-triage to ED/000 would be disproportionate.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Still unable to get fluids down despite the numbing paste, or pain not improving after starting topical treatment
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Within a few hours of trying the topical anaesthetic
escalation_edges.rationale: If topical analgesia does not restore the ability to drink, same-day in-person review and consideration of rehydration support are needed before dehydration progresses.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Try the numbing paste and then sip cool water. If you still can't drink after a couple of hours, contact the service today for a same-day review.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Worsening dizziness, feeling faint, no urine passed for more than 12 hours, or a fall
escalation_edges.rationale: These indicate significant dehydration requiring emergency department assessment and intravenous fluids; the risk is higher because the patient lives alone.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you feel very faint, haven't passed urine for over 12 hours, or you have a fall, go to the nearest Emergency Department.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Completely unable to swallow even your own saliva, or drooling
escalation_edges.rationale: Total inability to swallow saliva signals severe odynophagia with airway/hydration risk and needs emergency assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you get to the point where you cannot swallow your own saliva at all, go to the Emergency Department.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: New high fever or feeling systemically unwell
escalation_edges.rationale: Fever raises the possibility of secondary infection or an alternative cause and warrants urgent same-day reassessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you develop a high fever or feel generally very unwell, seek urgent medical review the same day.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: New eye redness/pain, any change in vision, or new genital sores
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Same day for eye symptoms; promptly for genital lesions
escalation_edges.rationale: In this recurrent-oral-ulcer spectrum, new ocular involvement is sight-threatening and new genital ulceration signals systemic evolution — both need urgent same-day/prompt specialist-directed review.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If your eyes become red or painful, your vision changes, or you get new sores in the genital area, get seen the same day and tell them about your recurrent mouth ulcers.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Lives alone with no one to help or monitor if symptoms worsen
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Lower the threshold to escalate and make the safety-net concrete: confirm the patient can reach fluids, knows exactly which symptoms mean 'call today' vs 'go to ED now', and can summon help; consider a same-day check-in on hydration.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: This needs urgent same-day attention, but it is not an emergency right now. Use a numbing paste on the ulcers, then sip cool, non-acidic fluids often to get your hydration back up, and get the anti-inflammatory (steroid) paste started today. Arrange a same-day telehealth or GP script for the paste if you don't already have it.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Urgent same-day telehealth or GP; after-hours medical service if outside clinic hours
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: If it is after hours, use an after-hours GP/home-doctor service or call healthdirect on 1800 022 222 for advice; go to the Emergency Department for any of the red-flag triggers.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Document the recurrent oral aphthae history, this severe flare with 12h reduced intake and orthostatic symptoms, maintained observations, topical therapy commenced, and the plan to confirm the underlying cause and screen for extra-oral involvement.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating the flare as trivial self-care and ignoring the 12h of no intake, orthostatic symptoms and living-alone risk.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Progression to significant dehydration, collapse or acute kidney injury at home with no one to intervene.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Advising only a routine 24-48h GP review without arranging same-day topical therapy and rehydration, risking another day without adequate intake.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Sending the patient to ED or calling an ambulance for a mild mucocutaneous flare with maintained haemodynamics and clear mentation — unnecessary ED burden and distress.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Whether the recurrent oral ulceration is benign recurrent aphthous stomatitis or the mild mucocutaneous form of a systemic autoimmune disease — undecidable at a single telehealth contact.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Treat the flare and the hydration risk (which is the same regardless of label), call the underlying cause presumptive, and arrange GP follow-up plus safety-netting for extra-oral evolution.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Either asserting a confirmed systemic diagnosis remotely, or dismissing the recurrence as trivial and skipping follow-up and ocular/systemic safety-netting.
